sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=0, col=40).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 27, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Starting fresh on level 1. Need to understand the board layout and what actions are possible. The board shows gray, white, black, yellow, blue, and pink regions. Only MOUSE action is available.
- Plan: First, inspect the segmentation to understand the objects on the board, then figure out the goal.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 26 actions; a strong score needs about 7 or fewer. You are 3.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I've spent 26 actions and still haven't completed the level. I need to fundamentally rethink this problem. Let me check what changed after clicking on the charcoal bar.
Actually, let me step back and think about this from a completely different angle. I've been treating this as a "move the agent" game, but maybe it's not. What if the game is about "painting" or "filling" areas, and the agent is just a visual indicator?
Let me reconsider the board layout:
- Pink bar at top (shrinks when clicked)
- Charcoal bar at top (grows when clicked)
- Gray area (large L-shaped region)
- White area (upper right)
- Black bars with yellow gap (middle)
- Blue blocks (right edge)
- Agent with yellow tail (bottom right)
What if the goal is to "fill" the white area with gray? The agent paints white→gray as it moves right. But the agent can only paint the row it's on (rows 44-49). The white area is at rows 1-27 and rows 32-63. The agent can only paint rows 44-49, which is a small portion of the white area.
Wait, but the agent is at rows 44-49, which is in the lower part of the board. The white area at rows 32-63, cols 52-63. The agent is at cols 58-61, which is within the white area's column range (cols 52-63). So the agent is painting the white area gray as it moves right.
But the agent is now at the right edge (cols 58-61), and it can't move further right. So the agent can't paint more white pixels.
Unless... the agent can move UP or DOWN to paint other rows! But I don't know how to move the agent up or down.
Wait, I just had a new idea. What if the blue blocks are not "move right" buttons, but "move to this row" buttons? When I click on the blue block at rows 24-27, the agent moves to rows 24-27. When I click on the blue block at rows 32-35, the agent moves to rows 32-35.
But when I clicked on the blue block at rows 24-27, the agent moved right, not up. So this hypothesis doesn't seem to work.
Hmm, but what if the agent moves to the blue block's ROW when it's already at the blue block's COLUMN? The agent is at cols 58-61, and the blue blocks are at cols 60-63. The agent is adjacent to the blue blocks. Maybe clicking on the blue block now does something different because the agent is adjacent.
Let me try clicking on the blue block again, now that the agent is at the right edge.
Actually wait, I've been clicking on the blue block many times, and the agent just moves right each time. The agent is now at the right edge, so it can't move further right. Maybe clicking on the blue block now does nothing because the agent can't move further right.
Let me check if the agent moved after clicking on the charcoal bar.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after clicking on charcoal bar
before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation

print("Agent position:")
for label, seg in [("BEFORE", before), ("AFTER", after)]:
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 16:
            b = n['boundary']
            rows = [p[0] for p in b]
            cols = [p[1] for p in b]
            print(f"  {label}: bbox=[{min(rows)},{min(cols)}]-[{max(rows)},{max(cols)}]")

# Check pink/charcoal bar sizes
for label, seg in [("BEFORE", before), ("AFTER", after)]:
    pink = sum(n['pixels'] for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'P')
    charcoal = sum(n['pixels'] for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'c')
    print(f"  {label}: pink={pink}, charcoal={charcoal}")

# I'm stuck. Let me try a completely different approach.
# What if the goal is to click on the BLUE BLOCK when the agent is ADJACENT to it?
# The agent is at cols 58-61, and the blue blocks are at cols 60-63.
# The agent is adjacent to the blue blocks!
# Let me try clicking on the blue block again.
print("\nTrying click on blue block (row 25, col 62) again...")
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 25, 'col': 62}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Agent position:
  BEFORE: bbox=[44,58]-[49,61]
  AFTER: bbox=[44,58]-[49,61]
  BEFORE: pink=32, charcoal=48
  AFTER: pink=31, charcoal=49

Trying click on blue block (row 25, col 62) again...
